---
title: "Week 1, Session 2 — Randomised controlled trials"
subtitle: "Course 3 — #courses"
---

::: {.callout-note}
**Inference lab** using the five-step template:
Hypothesis → Visualise → Assumptions → Conduct → Conclude.
:::

## Learning objectives

- Contrast parallel-group, crossover, cluster, and factorial RCT
  designs.
- Simulate a parallel-group RCT with a realistic allocation procedure.
- Interpret intention-to-treat (ITT) and per-protocol (PP) analyses
  and say why they can differ.

## Prerequisites

Course 1 inference; `t.test()`, `lm()`, and basic tidyverse.

## Background

The randomised controlled trial (RCT) is the reference design for
causal inference because randomisation balances both measured and
unmeasured confounders in expectation. Parallel-group RCTs assign
each participant to one arm for the duration of the study. Crossover
trials give each participant every treatment in a randomised order
and rely on a washout period. Cluster trials randomise groups
(clinics, villages) rather than individuals. Factorial trials vary
more than one treatment at once and can answer more than one
question per patient.

Every RCT has two analyses that will often disagree. Intention-to-
treat analyses everyone in the arm they were randomised to,
regardless of what they actually received. Per-protocol analyses
only those who received the assigned treatment as planned. ITT is
conservative for superiority and preserves the randomisation; PP is
informative for efficacy in compliant patients but can be biased.
Report both, and say which is primary in the protocol.

::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
Allocation concealment — the process that keeps the next assignment
unknown until the patient has been enrolled — is not the same as
blinding. You can have one without the other, and you need both for
the trial to protect itself from selection bias and differential
outcome ascertainment.
:::

## Setup

```#| label: setup
library(tidyverse)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))
```

## 1. Hypothesis

Treatment reduces the outcome (a continuous symptom score) relative
to placebo. We will also see what happens when 15% of participants
cross over from treatment to placebo after randomisation.

## 2. Visualise

```n <- 200
trial <- tibble(
  id    = seq_len(n),
  arm   = sample(rep(c("placebo", "treatment"), each = n / 2)),
  # 15% of treatment arm never take the drug (will be placebo in reality)
  crossed = if_else(arm == "treatment" & runif(n) < 0.15, TRUE, FALSE),
  received = if_else(crossed, "placebo", arm),
  y = rnorm(n, mean = 50, sd = 8) +
      if_else(received == "treatment", -5, 0)
)

trial |>
  ggplot(aes(arm, y, fill = arm)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6, colour = "grey30") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Symptom score") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

## 3. Assumptions

A two-sample *t*-test on the ITT population assumes independent
observations within arms and roughly normal residuals within each
arm. We do not assume equal variances; `t.test()` uses Welch by
default. The more important assumption is that randomisation worked —
that the allocation is independent of every baseline covariate.

```trial |>
  group_by(arm) |>
  summarise(mean = mean(y), sd = sd(y), n = n())
```

## 4. Conduct

```itt <- t.test(y ~ arm, data = trial)
pp  <- t.test(y ~ received, data = trial)
itt
pp
```

The ITT estimate is closer to zero than the PP estimate because the
crossed-over patients pull the treatment arm mean toward placebo.

## 5. Concluding statement

> In a simulated parallel-group RCT (*n* = 200), the ITT analysis
> showed a mean difference of
> `round(diff(itt$estimate) * -1, 1)` (95% CI:
> `round(-itt$conf.int[2], 1)` to `round(-itt$conf.int[1], 1)`)
> on the symptom score; a per-protocol analysis gave a larger
> estimated benefit of
> `round(diff(pp$estimate) * -1, 1)`, illustrating the usual
> direction of disagreement when non-adherence is informative.

::: {.notes}
The practical takeaway is that ITT protects the randomisation, PP
answers a different question, and reporting only the one that looks
good is a quiet form of bias.
:::

## Common pitfalls

- Calling a non-random allocation "randomised" because a coin was
  flipped after enrolment.
- Reporting only PP and calling it primary.
- Ignoring stratification variables in the analysis when they were
  used at randomisation.
- Cluster trials analysed as if individuals were independent.

## Further reading

- Schulz KF, Altman DG, Moher D (2010), *CONSORT 2010 Statement*.
- Pocock SJ. *Clinical Trials: A Practical Approach*.
- Hernán MA, Hernández-Díaz S (2012), *Beyond the intention-to-treat
  in comparative effectiveness research*.

## Session info

```sessionInfo()
```

<!-- BEGIN see-also (auto-injected) -->

## See also — chapter index

- [Methods in this chapter](../index.qmd)
- [Find your method (Chapter 0)](../../../ch00-find-your-method.qmd)

<!-- END see-also -->
